flashcards.Back: The developing unborn baby inside the uterus.
The organ where the fetus grows, responsible for labor and expulsion of the infant.
The vagina and the lower part of the uterus.
The fetal organ through which the fetus exchanges nourishment and waste products during pregnancy.
An extension of the placenta through which the fetus receives nourishment while in the uterus.
The fluid-filled bag of water that surrounds the fetus inside the uterus.
The lower part of the birth canal.
The skin area between the vagina and anus, which is commonly torn during delivery.
The bulging out of the vagina as the fetus's head or presenting part presses against it.
Mucus and blood that may come out of the vagina as labor begins.
The time and process beginning with the first uterine muscle contraction until delivery of the placenta.
The part of the infant or fetus that comes out first during delivery, usually the head.
The delivery of the products of conception early in pregnancy, also known as a spontaneous abortion.
The period extending from the beginning of the first uterine muscle contraction to full cervical dilation.
The stage of labor that begins with full cervical dilation and ends with the complete delivery of the infant.
The final stage of labor, which begins after the infant is born and ends with the delivery of the placenta.
Includes crowning, frequent contractions, the urge to push, and the feeling of a bowel movement.
Apply proper body substance isolation and have the mother lie with knees drawn up and spread apart.
Place fingers on the bony part of the infant's skull during crowning to apply gentle pressure.
If the sac has not broken during delivery, puncture it and push it away from the infant's face.
As the infant's head is born, check if the umbilical cord is wrapped around the neck.
Slip the cord over the shoulder, or clamp, cut, and unwrap it if wrapped tightly around the neck.
After the head is born, support it and suction the mouth two or three times, followed by the nostrils.
Support the infant's head and torso with both hands as the rest of the body is born.
Wipe blood and mucus from the infant's mouth and nose, then suction the mouth and nose again.
Wrap the infant in a warm blanket and keep them level with the vagina until the cord is cut.
Clamp, tie, and cut the cord between the clamps after internal pulsations cease.
Observe for the delivery of the placenta, wrap it in a towel, and place it in a plastic bag for transport.
Up to 500 cc of blood loss is well tolerated and considered normal following childbirth.
Manage excessive post-delivery bleeding by massaging the uterus with fingers fully extended over the lower abdomen.
Quickly position, dry, wipe, and wrap the newborn in a blanket, covering the head to prevent heat loss.
If the newborn is not breathing, stimulate them by flicking the soles of their feet or rubbing their back.
Assess the newborn's skin color, checking for the normal presence of pink skin and absence of central cyanosis.
Evaluate the newborn's heart rate, which should normally be greater than 100 beats per minute.
Observe the newborn's reflexes and irritability, looking for vigorous movement and crying.
Check the newborn's muscle tone, looking for good motion and active movement in the extremities.
Assess the newborn's breathing effort, which should be characterized by normal breathing and crying.
If breathing is shallow, slow, or absent, provide artificial ventilations at 60 breaths per minute.
If the newborn's heart rate drops below 100 beats per minute, provide artificial ventilations and reassess.
If the heart rate is less than 80 beats per minute and not responding to ventilations, start chest compressions.
If the heart rate is less than 60 beats per minute, immediately start chest compressions and artificial ventilations.
If central cyanosis is present with adequate breathing, administer oxygen close to the face at 10-15 L/min.
A serious emergency where the umbilical cord presents through the birth canal before the infant's head.
Position the mother with head down or buttocks raised, and insert a sterile gloved hand to relieve pressure on the cord.
An abnormal delivery where the infant's buttocks or lower extremities are the first part to deliver.
An emergency where a single limb of the infant protrudes from the birth canal, requiring immediate rapid transport.
Greenish or brownish-yellow amniotic fluid that indicates possible fetal distress during labor.
Do not stimulate the infant before suctioning the oropharynx to prevent the aspiration of contaminated fluid.
An infant born before full term, who is always at a high risk for hypothermia and often requires resuscitation.
Low blood pressure in a pregnant patient when lying flat, treated by positioning her on her left side.
